title: When You Claim
subtitle: The moment of truth — and why claims fail

hook.metaphor: Insurance is a promise you only test on the worst day of your life. And on that day, people discover the hard way that 'I have insurance' and 'I'm actually covered for *this*, for the *full amount*' are very different things. The claim is the moment the safety net is real or revealed to have a hole — and the holes were almost always cut, quietly, long before.
hook.scenario: After a fire, a business confidently files a claim — and is stunned when the insurer pays only a fraction. It turns out they were under-insured, hadn't disclosed a key change, and the cause fell under an exclusion. They 'had insurance'. So why are they still nearly ruined?

learn: A **claim** is the process of asking the insurer to pay for a covered loss: notify them, document what happened, and substantiate the amount. It's the entire point of insurance — and the moment that exposes whether the cover was actually adequate.

Claims get **reduced or denied** for predictable reasons, all visible in our scenario: the loss fell under an **exclusion** (not covered — read the fine print, Level 2); the policyholder breached a **condition** or failed to **disclose** something material (insurers can reduce or void cover for non-disclosure); or the claim was poorly **documented/substantiated**.

The most common quiet killer is **underinsurance**: insuring an asset for less than its true rebuild/replacement value. Many policies then apply 'average', paying out *proportionally* less — insure for half the value and you may get roughly half of even a partial loss. People discover this only at claim time.

Two duties matter throughout: **disclosure** (telling the insurer the truth, and updating them on material changes — a new high-risk activity, a moved location) and **documentation** (keeping records, valuations, and evidence so you can prove both that you're covered and how much you lost). A claim is won or lost on evidence (the assurance thread, again).

The deep lesson: the time to ensure a claim will succeed is **when you buy and maintain the policy**, not when disaster strikes. Reading exclusions, insuring to full value, disclosing changes, and keeping records are all done *beforehand*. On the worst day, it's far too late to fix the cover.
bridge: So at work, you can make insurance actually pay out by doing the work before the claim: *are we insured to full value (not under-insured)? have we disclosed everything material and kept it updated? do we understand the exclusions? and can we document a loss if it happens?* 'I have insurance' is only as good as those answers — the holes in the safety net are cut at purchase, and only discovered at the claim.

interactions.title: Insured, yet nearly ruined
interactions.prompt: After a fire, the insurer pays only a fraction — the firm was under-insured, hadn't disclosed a change, and the cause was excluded. The core lesson?

options.label: The insurer is just cheating them.
options.feedback: It's not cheating — each reduction traces to a real gap: underinsurance, non-disclosure, and an exclusion. The holes were cut at purchase; the firm just discovered them at claim time.

options.label: Adequate cover is set before the loss — value, disclosure, and exclusions all matter up front.
options.feedback: Exactly. 'Having insurance' isn't 'being covered for this, in full'. Insure to full value, disclose material changes, and understand exclusions — beforehand, because the worst day is too late to fix it.

options.label: They should have just claimed more confidently.
options.feedback: Confidence doesn't override exclusions, underinsurance, or non-disclosure. The payout is governed by the policy terms and your prior actions, not how firmly you ask.

interactions.title: Why claims get cut
interactions.prompt: Sort each factor by whether it strengthens or undermines a claim.

items.label: Insuring the asset to its full replacement value
Disclosing all material facts and changes
The loss falling under an exclusion
Good documentation and evidence of the loss
Failing to disclose a key change

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “A claim is the moment the safety net is tested — and it fails for predictable reasons (exclusions, non-disclosure, underinsurance, poor documentation) whose holes were cut at purchase, so you secure the payout when you buy and maintain the policy, not when disaster strikes.”

options: It always pays out in full
Insuring below true value can mean a proportionally reduced payout ('average')
It has no effect on claims
checkpoint.explain: Insure for half the value and you may receive roughly half even of a partial loss — discovered at claim time.

checkpoint.q: A common, legitimate reason a claim is denied is…
options: The loss falls under an exclusion or a material fact wasn't disclosed
The claim was filed too confidently
checkpoint.explain: Exclusions, breached conditions, and non-disclosure are the predictable claim-killers in the fine print.

checkpoint.q: The best time to ensure a claim will succeed is…
options: When you buy and maintain the policy — value, disclosure, exclusions, records
Never — it's out of your hands
checkpoint.explain: The holes in cover are cut at purchase; on the worst day it's far too late to fix them.
